summary:
South Africa's first fully democratic elections, held on April 27–29, 1994, ended the apartheid system and constituted one of the most celebrated political transitions of the twentieth century. The elections were the culmination of a negotiated transition that had begun with the legalization of the African National Congress and other banned organizations in February 1990 and the release of Nelson Mandela after 27 years of imprisonment. Four years of multiparty negotiations produced a new constitution guaranteeing universal suffrage and equal rights.

Voting took place over three days to accommodate the estimated twenty million first-time voters. Long queues of South Africans from all backgrounds waiting peacefully to cast their first ballot created iconic images that circulated globally. The ANC won 62.6 percent of the vote, giving it a large majority in the new National Assembly. Nelson Mandela was elected South Africa's first Black president and was inaugurated on May 10, 1994, in a ceremony attended by world leaders.

Mandela's inauguration and his subsequent presidency were marked by a spirit of reconciliation rather than retribution. His government established the [[event:3490|Truth and Reconciliation Commission]], chaired by Archbishop Desmond Tutu, to address the crimes of the apartheid era through testimony and conditional amnesty rather than prosecution. This approach, though controversial, was widely seen as crucial to the peaceful nature of the transition and became influential as a model for other post-conflict societies.
